title: What kind of news reader are you?
description: Rate how much you agree or disagree with each statement. Answer instinctively — your first reaction is the most honest one. We'll show you your reader archetype at the end.

archetypes.name: The Owl
archetypes.tagline: Wise, watchful, and slow to judge.
archetypes.description: You verify before believing, stay calm in the face of dramatic headlines, and read across the spectrum. You're the kind of reader misinformation has a hard time fooling. The risk: getting so cautious that you disengage entirely, or assuming everyone less rigorous than you is foolish.

archetypes.name: The Sentinel
archetypes.tagline: Deeply informed in your lane, quiet outside it.
archetypes.description: You're a careful, calm thinker — but within a narrow set of sources or topics. When something falls outside your usual reading, you're either silent or surprisingly easy to mislead. The risk: confident takes on subjects you haven't actually researched.

archetypes.name: The Scholar
archetypes.tagline: Reads widely, cares deeply, double-checks everything.
archetypes.description: You take in news from many directions and verify the claims that matter to you — but you're emotionally invested. Your strength is rigour; your risk is that emotional weight can pull your fact-checking off-balance. Watch for: investigating harder when facts align with your feelings.

archetypes.name: The Crusader
archetypes.description: You dig deeply, verify thoroughly, and care fiercely — but within a single cause or worldview. Within your domain you're formidable. Outside it, you may dismiss whole categories of sources without examining them. The risk: treating opposing arguments as unworthy of fact-checking.

archetypes.name: The Diplomat
archetypes.tagline: Open to everything, cautious of nothing.
archetypes.description: You read widely and stay calm — admirable traits — but you take what you read more or less at face value. You're charitable to all sides, which means misinformation from any direction can slip past you. The risk: assuming that balance equals accuracy.

archetypes.name: The Loyalist
archetypes.tagline: Trusts the trusted, stays in the lane.
archetypes.description: You're calm, consistent, and faithful to your usual sources. That trust feels well-placed because the sources reinforce each other — but you rarely test it. The risk: you'd believe almost anything your familiar outlets told you, without ever needing to verify it.

archetypes.name: The Megaphone
archetypes.tagline: Widely read, fast to feel, faster to share.
archetypes.description: You consume news from everywhere and react strongly to what moves you. Your breadth protects you from echo chambers, but your speed of response means you often amplify things before they're verified. The risk: the difference between caring about something and being right about it.

archetypes.name: The Cyclone
archetypes.tagline: Easily moved, narrowly fed, quick to spread.
archetypes.description: You trust your sources, feel news intensely, and stay within a familiar circle. This is the profile most easily targeted by misinformation — not because you're foolish, but because every layer of defence is down. The good news: each of these is a skill you can build. Watch for: any story that makes you immediately want to share it.

questions.statement: When reading a long investigative article, I frequently open new tabs to look up the background of the people being quoted.
I prefer news summaries or newsletters that give me the bottom line without making me wade through the underlying data.
I usually skip past the headline and opening paragraphs of an article to find the actual data embedded further down.
If a highly respected journalist publishes a story, I assume they have already done the rigorous fact-checking so I don't have to.
I have a habit of checking the publication date on articles or videos before I share them or react to them.
If a statistic confirms something I already strongly suspected, I rarely feel the need to verify it.
I pay close attention to whether a news article uses words like 'allegedly' or 'reportedly'.
When a major breaking news event happens, I trust the first accounts that come out from people on the ground.
I often look up who funds the think tanks or research groups cited in news articles.
If a video is circulating widely on a platform I trust, it is usually safe to assume it has not been deepfaked or altered.
I have actively deleted or retracted a post I made after realizing the information in it was outdated or incorrect.
The most valuable journalism is the kind that inspires people to take immediate action against injustice.
I find myself irritated when a journalist uses highly descriptive, passionate language to report on a political event.
If a news story doesn't make me feel something—whether anger, hope, or sadness—it probably isn't covering an issue that matters.
I actively distrust headlines that use highly charged adjectives like 'eviscerates', 'destroys', or 'humiliates'.
I am drawn to opinion writers who express a sense of righteous anger about the state of the world.
I prefer news anchors who deliver tragic or shocking news with a completely flat, emotionless tone.
When I see a story about a clear moral wrong, I feel an urgent need to ensure my social circle knows about it.
I intentionally wait a few days before reading about a controversial event so the emotional outrage has time to die down.
The best political commentators are the ones who aren't afraid to show their disgust at bad policies.
I often view attempts to make me feel empathy for a subject as a manipulation tactic by the author.
Apathy is a bigger problem in news consumption today than overreaction.
My browser history or podcast feed routinely contains completely contradictory perspectives on the exact same political event.
With so much misinformation out there, it is much safer to stick to two or three highly respected publications.
I sometimes purposefully seek out the opinions of commentators who actively annoy me.
I have curated my social media feeds so that I don't have to see posts from people whose political views I find abhorrent.
I subscribe to or regularly visit at least one news outlet that caters to a political demographic I do not belong to.
There are certain mainstream news outlets that I flat out refuse to click on, regardless of what they are covering.
I enjoy reading 'devil's advocate' arguments even on topics where I have deeply held moral convictions.
I rely primarily on a specific group of like-minded friends or specific forums to filter the news for me.
When reading an opinion piece I agree with, I actively try to think of the strongest counterargument the other side would make.
Life is too short to spend time understanding the perspectives of people who are clearly on the wrong side of history.
